steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Text Structure and Purpose question. The decisive clue is the opening simile — Louis "tended the petition like a garden" — combined with a list of everywhere he corners people to sign: at the gas pump, the mercantile, Henry's Café, on the road, outside the clinic. Every sentence in the passage adds detail about Louis's own relentless effort, never about anyone else's feelings or the community's overall reaction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — To show that attitudes toward the petition within the tribal community change over time: The passage records no shift in attitude; it describes one ongoing behavior (Louis pursuing signers). ✗
  • Choice B — To demonstrate that most tribal members are enthusiastic about signing the petition: The passage never evaluates members' enthusiasm; it focuses entirely on Louis's drive, not theirs. ✗
  • Choice C — To portray Louis Pipestone's strong commitment to collecting signatures for the petition: Every detail — keeping the petition at all times, cornering people in every situation — builds a portrait of Louis's personal dedication. ✓
  • Choice D — To suggest that some tribal members refuse to sign the petition because they dislike Louis Pipestone: The passage never mentions refusal or anyone disliking Louis; it implies he succeeds in getting signatures everywhere he goes. ✗
That matches Choice C — To portray Louis Pipestone's strong commitment to collecting signatures for the petition.
